name: van Paassen, M. M.
recent_1: Chen, J., Landman, A., Derumigny, A., Stroosma, O., van Paassen, M. M., & Mulder, M. (2025). Preliminary Multilevel Confirmatory Factor Analysis of the Startle and Surprise Inventories using Simulated Flight Scenarios. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. DOI: 10.1177/15553434251401784
recent_1_synopsis: This study used multilevel confirmatory factor analysis on data from simulated flight scenarios to validate the psychometric structure of the Startle and Surprise Inventories. It examined whether these self-report instruments reliably distinguish startle from surprise. The work strengthens the measurement foundations for pilot training and unexpected-event research.
recent_2: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2025). Startle and surprise in helicopter operations: reported prevalence and application of mitigation strategies. Cognition, Technology & Work, 27(3), 579-590. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-025-00811-y
recent_2_synopsis: A survey of 234 helicopter pilots found that 96% had experienced impactful startle or surprise events, with disorientation, tail-rotor incidents, and inadvertent entry into instrument meteorological conditions rated most stressful. The study assesses current mitigation strategies and the usability of the "Aviate, Breathe, Check" startle-management procedure. It extends the flight-deck startle/surprise program into rotary-wing operations.
recent_3: Evertsen, F. W., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., Stroosma, O., van Paassen, M. M., & Mulder, M. (2025). Quantifying the Impact of Spatial Disorientation on Pilot Mental Workload and Attentional Focus. Human Factors, 67(10), 997-1010. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251323116
recent_3_synopsis: This study empirically quantified how spatial disorientation elevates pilot mental workload and reshapes attentional allocation during simulated flight. By linking a perceptual illusion to objective workload and attention measures, it clarifies the cognitive cost of disorientation events. The findings inform training and display design aimed at helping pilots detect and recover from disorientation.
seminal_1: Lam, T. M., Boschloo, H. W., Mulder, M., & van Paassen, M. M. (2009). Artificial Force Field for Haptic Feedback in UAV Teleoperation. IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics - Part A: Systems and Humans, 39(6), 1316-1330. DOI: 10.1109/TSMCA.2009.2028239
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper introduced an artificial-force-field method that maps environmental obstacle constraints to repulsive haptic forces on the operator's control device, offloading the visual channel during UAV teleoperation. With roughly 230+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is his single most-cited work. It is foundational because it became a widely cited template for haptic shared-control and collision-avoidance feedback in unmanned and teleoperated vehicle research.
seminal_2: Amelink, M. H. J., Mulder, M., van Paassen, M. M., & Flach, J. (2005). Theoretical Foundations for a Total Energy-Based Perspective Flight-Path Display. The International Journal of Aviation Psychology, 15(3), 205-231. DOI: 10.1207/s15327108ijap1503_1
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper derived the total-energy reference profile and energy-angle constructs and applied Ecological Interface Design and the Abstraction Hierarchy to the aircraft energy-management and manual-control task. With roughly 130+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is an early, heavily cited demonstration of Ecological Interface Design applied to aircraft display design, anchoring a long program on ecological flight-deck interfaces.
expertise_overview: Research studies human-machine interaction in aerospace and vehicle control, spanning perceptual and cognitive processes, human manual control modeling, and cybernetics. Methods combine human-in-the-loop flight simulation (including the SIMONA research simulator and real-time/distributed simulation software), control-theoretic and genetic maximum-likelihood identification of operator behavior, and controlled experimental studies of pilot performance. A major thread applies Ecological Interface Design and the Abstraction Hierarchy to flight-deck and air-traffic displays, including energy-management, separation-assistance, and decision-support interfaces. A second major line concerns haptic and shared control—artificial force fields, haptic gas pedals, and haptic feedback for teleoperation and driving. More recent work addresses pilot training for startle and surprise, spatial disorientation, mental workload, and attentional focus under unexpected in-flight events. Application domains center on aviation (fixed- and rotary-wing piloting, flight simulation, air traffic control) and extend to UAV teleoperation and automotive driver support.
notes: High-confidence identification. Marinus M. (René) van Paassen, Control & Simulation section, Faculty of Aerospace Engineering, TU Delft; identity confirmed via the TU Delft staff page and Research Portal, Google Scholar (user=3nWdbGgAAAAJ), ResearchGate, the Delft Haptics Lab, and dblp, and anchored by the provided hf_papers and a stable co-author cluster (Mulder, Stroosma, Borst, Landman, Groen). Name normalized to "van Paassen, M. M." per Dutch convention (the "van" prefix stays with the surname). Independently verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 (HTTP 200, matching titles): 10.1177/15553434251401784, 10.1007/s10111-025-00811-y, 10.1177/00187208251323116, 10.1109/TSMCA.2009.2028239, 10.1207/s15327108ijap1503_1. No DOIs were invented; none were unverifiable. Seminal citation counts (~230+ and ~130+) are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 (profile totals ~5,278 citations, h-index 39).
